explicit_text: It was wintertime when I drove over Donner Summit in some pretty hairy weather to enter the Navy V-5 program. That drive marked the beginning of my journey into naval aviation training. The harsh conditions made the experience memorable as I was eager to join before being drafted.

explicit_text: I joined the Navy V-5 program to have some control over my destiny before the draft caught me. In V-5, I became a cadet and took the flying course, which included basic training at Oakland and advanced flight training in Corpus Christi, Texas. It was a rigorous program that prepared me for combat flying during World War II.

explicit_text: After entering the Navy V-5 program, I went through introductory basic training in Oakland, California. It was the first step before heading to Corpus Christi for serious flight training. Oakland was where I adjusted to military life and discipline.

explicit_text: Corpus Christi, Texas, was where I spent nine months undergoing serious flight training after basic training in Oakland. explicit_text: We practiced flying in a Link trainer, which was like a little plane mounted on a box that drew your course for the instructor to see. It was mentally maddening because the enlisted instructors didn’t care much about what we did, and there was a lot of horsing around. The trainer simulated rough air and spins, which made the training both challenging and sometimes funny.

explicit_text: After a year of flight training, I did carrier landings on Chesapeake Bay before heading to Rhode Island. That experience was part of my preparation to join Bombing Squadron 42 aboard the USS Ranger. Chesapeake Bay was where I first practiced the demanding skill of carrier landings.

explicit_text: I joined Bombing Squadron 42 aboard the USS Ranger in Rhode Island after completing my training. Our squadron had thirty planes and forty pilots, and we flew anti-submarine patrols in the Atlantic, including missions near Newfoundland. It was a mix of boredom and occasional excitement, but it was my first real taste of naval combat operations.

explicit_text: After leaving the Atlantic, we sailed six days from San Diego to Hilo, Hawaii, where we practiced with our new Curtiss Helldiver planes. Hilo was a key stop before moving on to Saipan and joining the USS Bunker Hill. It was a transition point from training to active combat in the Pacific.

There was a lot of drinking at the bars, where they made you buy a case of beer instead of just one. Saipan was our staging area before joining the USS Bunker Hill and entering combat.

I had just completed aerial reconnaissance photo school and managed to get my gunner to carry a camera on missions. That month was intense, with attacks on enemy ships and the constant danger of being shot down.

explicit_text: After being shot down near Manila Bay, my gunner and I drifted out into the China Sea on a small raft.
